text: Recently, the Ecuadorian State acknowledged the need to invest in the infrastructure and human capital in order to promote the principle of educational inclusion—a principle that in practice separates developed countries from the developing ones. The purpose of this study was to diagnose international regulations and practices regarding the school infrastructure as a way of identifying possible knowledge gaps and possible adaptation roads in the context of Ecuador. Applying the PRISMA-ScR recommendations, an exploratory review was carried out in the main scientific databases and governmental regulations in the United States, Finland, Canada, and Japan that resulted in the analysis of 16 scientific articles and 11 regulatory documents. The findings show that in Ecuador there are obstacles at the system level in the infrastructure of the physical and digital educational space, thus requiring the national regulatory framework to include accessibility standards, adequate public spending in the use of assistive technologies, and continuous teacher training. Beyond these recommendations, it was found that there is a vicious circle of non-compliance with the regulations, which perpetuates social inequality and makes it more difficult to modify the educational institutions’ behaviour. Therefore, the Ecuadorian State should tackle the change in an integral way at the level of regulation, resource planning, and institution-building institutions.

Beyond these recommendations, it was found that there is a vicious circle of non-compliance with the regulations, which perpetuates social inequality and makes it more difficult to modify the educational institutions’ behaviour.
